candidates.name: Inversion
candidates.description: Jacobi's 'invert, always invert' applied to problem-solving: instead of asking how to succeed, ask what causes failure and avoid it. Mathematical inversion mapped onto strategic reasoning.

candidates.name: Circle of Competence
candidates.description: Geometric boundary (circle) mapped onto knowledge domains. Knowing the edge of your circle matters more than its size. Munger's most personal model.

candidates.name: Margin of Safety
candidates.description: Engineering safety margins (bridge load capacity) mapped onto investment decisions. Build in a buffer because your estimates are always wrong. From Graham, central to Munger-Buffett method.

candidates.name: Incentive-Caused Bias
candidates.description: Economic incentive theory mapped onto cognitive bias. 'Never think about something else when you should be thinking about the power of incentives.' Munger's #1 psychology tendency.

candidates.name: Lollapalooza Effect
candidates.description: Physics concept of resonance and wave superposition mapped onto psychology: when multiple cognitive biases align and reinforce each other, producing extreme outcomes. Munger's coinage.

candidates.name: Man with a Hammer
candidates.description: Tool-use frame mapped onto reasoning: 'To a man with a hammer, everything looks like a nail.' The anti-model that motivates the whole latticework. Munger's argument for multidisciplinary thinking.

candidates.name: Second-Order Thinking
candidates.description: Physics concept of second-order derivatives mapped onto consequence analysis. First-order: what happens next? Second-order: and then what? The distinction between chess players and checkers players.

candidates.name: Mr. Market
candidates.description: Personification of the stock market as a manic-depressive business partner who shows up daily with offers. Graham's allegory, central to Munger-Buffett investing philosophy.

candidates.name: Economic Moats
candidates.description: Medieval castle defense (moat) mapped onto competitive advantage. Width and depth of the moat determine durability of business returns. Buffett's term, Munger's analytical framework.

candidates.name: Latticework of Mental Models
candidates.description: Architectural lattice structure mapped onto knowledge organization. Models must interconnect, not sit in isolation. The meta-model that organizes all other models. Munger's signature concept.

candidates.name: Compounding
candidates.description: Financial compound interest mapped onto knowledge, relationships, reputation. 'The first rule of compounding: never interrupt it unnecessarily.' Extends far beyond money.

candidates.name: Feedback Loops
candidates.description: Control systems engineering (positive and negative feedback) mapped onto business and social dynamics. Positive loops amplify; negative loops stabilize. Understanding which type is operating changes everything.

candidates.name: Critical Mass
candidates.description: Nuclear physics threshold concept mapped onto social and business tipping points. Below critical mass, nothing happens. Above it, chain reaction. The difference between a fizzle and an explosion.

candidates.name: Network Effects
candidates.description: Telecommunications network value (Metcalfe's law) mapped onto business competitive advantage. Each new user increases value for all existing users. The flywheel that creates winner-take-all markets.

candidates.name: Red Queen Effect
candidates.description: Evolutionary biology concept (via Carroll's Alice) mapped onto competitive dynamics: you must run just to stay in place. Adaptation is continuous, not a destination.

candidates.name: Niche Specialization
candidates.description: Ecological niche theory mapped onto business strategy. Species thrive by occupying specific niches; so do businesses. Competing in someone else's niche is usually fatal.

candidates.name: Leverage
candidates.description: Mechanical advantage (lever and fulcrum) mapped onto business and finance. Small force, large effect -- but leverage amplifies both gains and losses. 'Give me a lever long enough and I shall move the world.'

candidates.name: Activation Energy
candidates.archive_ref: fs.blog; chemistry concept applied by Munger
candidates.description: Chemical reaction threshold mapped onto behavior change and habit formation. Every change requires an initial energy input that exceeds a threshold. Explains why good habits are hard to start and bad ones hard to stop.

candidates.name: Catalysts
candidates.archive_ref: fs.blog; chemistry concept applied by Munger
candidates.description: Chemical catalysis mapped onto change agents. A catalyst accelerates a reaction without being consumed. In business: the person, event, or policy that unlocks latent change.

candidates.name: Principal-Agent Problem
candidates.description: Economic agency theory mapped onto organizational governance. When the agent's incentives diverge from the principal's, the agent serves themselves. Munger considers this one of the most important models in business.

candidates.name: Opportunity Cost
candidates.description: Economic cost theory mapped onto general decision-making. The true cost of anything is what you give up to get it. Munger: 'The wise ones bet heavily when the world offers them that opportunity.'

candidates.name: Regression to the Mean
candidates.description: Statistical phenomenon mapped onto prediction and judgment. Extreme results tend to be followed by more moderate ones. Mistaking regression for causation is one of the most common reasoning errors.

candidates.name: Power Laws
candidates.description: Mathematical distribution mapped onto real-world outcome patterns. A small number of inputs produce the majority of outputs. The 80/20 rule is just one instance. Explains why averages are often misleading.

candidates.name: Bayesian Updating
candidates.description: Probability theorem mapped onto belief revision. Update your prior beliefs proportionally to the strength of new evidence. The formal version of 'change your mind when the facts change.'

candidates.name: Social Proof
candidates.description: Herd behavior (evolved for survival) mapped onto modern decision-making. People look to others' actions to determine correct behavior. Munger: one of the 25 standard causes of misjudgment.

candidates.name: Reciprocity
candidates.description: Economic exchange mapped onto social dynamics. Humans feel compelled to return favors and match hostility. One of the deepest human behavioral patterns. Exploitable and exploited constantly.

candidates.name: Creative Destruction
candidates.description: Biological death-and-renewal cycle mapped onto market dynamics. Innovation destroys incumbents. Munger: 'The great lesson in microeconomics is to discriminate between when technology is going to help you and when it's going to kill you.'

candidates.name: Switching Costs
candidates.description: Physical friction/inertia mapped onto customer behavior and competitive advantage. The higher the cost to leave, the more durable the business. A key component of Munger's moat analysis.

candidates.name: Scale Economies
candidates.description: Physical scaling laws mapped onto cost structure. As volume increases, unit cost decreases -- but only to a point. Munger distinguishes supply-side (spreading fixed costs) from demand-side (network effects) scale.

candidates.name: Redundancy
candidates.description: Engineering backup systems mapped onto organizational and personal resilience. Multiple independent failure points prevent catastrophic collapse. The opposite of efficiency optimization taken too far.

candidates.name: First Principles Thinking
candidates.description: Physics methodology (derive from axioms, not analogy) mapped onto general reasoning. Strip away assumptions until you reach foundational truths, then build up. Munger and Musk both advocate this.

candidates.name: Occam's Razor
candidates.archive_ref: sourcesofinsight #44; fs.blog; William of Ockham c.1320
candidates.description: Razor (cutting tool) metaphor applied to explanation selection. Among competing hypotheses, prefer the simplest. Not because simple is always right, but because unnecessary complexity is usually wrong.

candidates.name: Hanlon's Razor
candidates.description: Another razor metaphor: never attribute to malice what is adequately explained by stupidity. Cuts away paranoid explanations. Deeply practical for organizational life.

candidates.name: Falsification
candidates.description: Scientific method mapped onto everyday reasoning. Seek disconfirming evidence, not confirming evidence. Munger: 'I never allow myself to have an opinion on anything that I don't know the other side's argument better than they do.'

candidates.name: Two-Track Analysis
candidates.description: Dual-track engineering inspection mapped onto decision analysis. Track 1: what are the rational factors? Track 2: what psychological biases are at play? Munger's signature analytical method.

candidates.name: Scenario Analysis
candidates.description: Military war-gaming mapped onto business and investment planning. Instead of predicting one future, map out several plausible futures and prepare for each. Reduces overconfidence in single-point forecasts.

candidates.name: Cancer Surgery Formula
candidates.description: Surgical excision mapped onto business turnarounds. Cut out the cancerous division quickly and completely rather than hoping it will heal. Preserve healthy tissue. Delay makes it worse.

candidates.name: Comparative Advantage
candidates.description: International trade theory mapped onto personal and organizational strategy. Even if you are better at everything, specialization and trade make everyone better off. The basis for delegation and focus.

candidates.name: Information Asymmetry
candidates.description: Market for lemons theory mapped onto general negotiation and trust. When one party knows more than the other, markets malfunction. Explains why Munger values transparent management so highly.

candidates.name: Nonlinearity
candidates.description: Mathematical non-linear functions mapped onto real-world cause-and-effect. Small inputs can produce huge outputs, and vice versa. Linear thinking is the default; reality is mostly non-linear.

candidates.name: System Resilience vs. Fragility
candidates.description: Structural engineering concepts mapped onto organizational and financial systems. Some systems break under stress; others absorb it; the best gain from it (antifragility). Munger's investment approach seeks resilient systems.

candidates.name: Culture as a Control System
candidates.description: Engineering control systems (thermostats, governors) mapped onto organizational culture. Norms and values regulate behavior more effectively than rules. Munger prizes Berkshire's culture over its written policies.

candidates.name: Checklist Approach
candidates.description: Aviation and surgical checklists mapped onto investment and decision analysis. Simple lists compensate for human cognitive limits. Munger: 'No wise pilot, no matter how great his talent and experience, fails to use a checklist.'
